items.name: Hammer
items.text: A hammer is one of the oldest tools humans ever made. It has a heavy metal head and a long handle to hold. We can use it to hit nails into wood when building things.

items.name: Screwdriver
items.text: A screwdriver is used to turn screws in and out. It has a handle and a thin metal shaft with a different tip. The two most common types are flat-head and Phillips (cross-shaped).

items.name: Ruler
items.text: A ruler is a flat strip used to measure length and draw straight lines. Most rulers are 30 cm (12 inches) long. They can be made of wood, plastic, or metal.

items.name: Pliers
items.text: It is a gripping tool used to hold, bend, and cut things. They have two handles and two jaws that squeeze together. Electricians use pliers to bend and cut wires safely.

items.name: Drill
items.text: A drill is a tool that spins a sharp bit to make holes. Electric drills are the most common. They are fast and powerful. Drills are used to make holes in wood, metal, and walls.

items.name: Spirit Level
items.text: A spirit level is a tool used to check if a surface is perfectly flat or straight. Inside it is a sealed tube with liquid and a small air bubble. When the bubble sits exactly between two lines. The surface is level!

items.name: Chisel
items.text: A chisel is a cutting tool with a sharp flat blade at the end. You hit the top of it with a mallet to carve or cut. Woodworkers use chisels to carve beautiful shapes into wood.

items.name: Wrench
items.text: A wrench is a tool used to grip and turn nuts and bolts. It is also known as a spanner in many countries including India. There are open-end wrenches, box wrenches, and adjustable wrenches.

items.name: Saw
items.text: A saw is a cutting tool with a row of sharp teeth along its blade. You move it back and forth to cut through wood, metal, or plastic. The handsaw is the simplest. Just a blade with a handle.

items.name: Tape Measure
items.text: A tape measure is a flexible ruler coiled inside a small case. You pull the metal tape out to measure long distances. Most tape measures are 3 to 10 metres long.

items.name: Sandpaper
items.text: It is a rough paper coated with tiny grains of abrasive material. You rub it on surfaces to make them smooth. The number on sandpaper tells you how rough it is. Lower numbers are rougher.

items.name: Clamp
items.text: A clamp is a device used to hold two things tightly together. Carpenters use clamps to hold pieces of wood while the glue dries. Without a clamp the glued parts would slide apart before they set.

items.name: Paint Brush
items.text: A paint brush is used to apply paint, varnish, or glue to surfaces. Brushes come in many sizes. Small for detail work, large for big areas. The bristles are the hairy part that holds and spreads the paint.

items.name: Torque Wrench
items.text: A torque wrench is a different tool used to tighten bolts and nuts to an exact force. It makes sure the bolt is not too loose or too tight. Mechanics use it when fixing cars, trucks, and motorbikes.

items.name: Black Hole
items.text: A black hole is a place in space where gravity is very strong. Not even light can escape from it. That is why it looks black! Black holes form when a very large star runs out of fuel and collapses.

items.name: Milky Way
items.text: The Milky Way is the galaxy we live in. It is a huge spiral shape with billions of stars. On a clear night away from city lights, you can see it as a faint band across the sky.

items.name: Nebula
items.text: A nebula is a giant cloud of gas and dust floating in space. The name nebula comes from Latin and means cloud. Nebulae are the birthplaces of new stars!

items.name: Comet
items.text: A comet is a small icy body that moves through space. When it gets close to the Sun, the ice melts and forms a glowing tail. The tail always points away from the Sun because of solar wind.

items.name: Saturn
items.text: It is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second largest in our Solar System. It is famous for its stunning rings made of ice and rock. It is so light it would actually float on water!

items.name: Mars
items.text: It is the fourth planet from the Sun and our closest neighbour. It is called the Red Planet because of its reddish iron-rich dust. It has the tallest volcano in the Solar System. Olympus Mons.

items.name: The Moon
items.text: It is Earth's only natural satellite and our closest neighbour in space. It is about 384,000 kilometres away from Earth. It has no air, no weather, and no sound.

items.name: Sun
items.text: The Sun is the star at the centre of our Solar System. It is a giant ball of hot gas. Mostly hydrogen and helium. The Sun is so large that about 1.3 million Earths could fit inside it.

items.name: Jupiter
items.text: It is the largest planet in our Solar System. It is so big that all the other planets could fit inside it! It is a gas giant. It has no solid surface to stand on.

items.name: Northern Lights
items.text: The Northern Lights, or Aurora Borealis, are one of nature's most stunning displays. They appear as colourful ribbons of light dancing across the night sky. They happen when charged particles from the Sun hit Earth's atmosphere.

items.name: International Space Station
items.text: The International Space Station, or ISS, is a large spacecraft orbiting Earth. It flies about 400 kilometres above us. Travelling at 28,000 km per hour. At that speed it orbits Earth 16 times every single day!

items.name: Shooting Star
items.text: A shooting star is not actually a star. It is a tiny piece of rock or dust from space. When it enters Earth's atmosphere, it burns up due to friction with the air. This burning creates the bright streak of light we see in the sky.

items.name: Supernova
items.text: A supernova is a very big explosion that happens when a large star dies. It is the most powerful explosion in the universe. A supernova can briefly shine brighter than an entire galaxy of billions of stars!

items.name: Asteroid Belt
items.text: The Asteroid Belt is a region of space between Mars and Jupiter. It contains millions of rocky and metallic objects known as asteroids. Asteroids are leftovers from the formation of our Solar System 4.6 billion years ago.

items.name: Airplane
items.text: An airplane is a flying machine that carries people and goods through the air. It has wings that help lift it up when it moves fast. The first airplane was built by the Wright Brothers in 1903.

items.name: Submarine
items.text: A submarine is a different ship that can travel underwater. It dives below the surface by filling tanks with water to become heavier. To come back up, it pushes the water out with air.

items.name: Helicopter
items.text: A helicopter is an aircraft that can fly in any direction. Even hover in one spot! Instead of wings, it uses spinning blades on top known as rotors. The rotors spin very fast and push air downward to lift the helicopter up.

items.name: Bicycle
items.text: A bicycle is a two-wheeled machine powered by pedalling with your feet. When you push the pedals, a chain turns the back wheel. Bicycles do not need petrol. They run entirely on human energy!

items.name: Rocket
items.text: A rocket is a machine that uses burning fuel to propel itself through space. Unlike airplanes, rockets carry their own oxygen. So they work in space where there is no air. The burning fuel creates hot gases that shoot out the back, pushing the rocket forward.

items.name: Train
items.text: A train is a machine that runs on steel rails and carries passengers or goods. The first steam train was invented by George Stephenson in 1825. Modern trains are powered by electricity or diesel engines.

items.name: Ship
items.text: Ships are large water vessels that carry people and cargo across oceans. They have been used for thousands of years for exploration and trade. The Titanic was one of the most famous ships. It sank in 1912 after hitting an iceberg.

items.name: Tractor
items.text: A tractor is a powerful machine used mainly on farms. It has huge rear wheels and a strong engine to pull heavy loads. Farmers attach different tools. Ploughs, harrows, and seeders. To the back.

items.name: Ambulance
items.text: An ambulance is a different machine used to transport sick or injured people to hospital. It carries medical equipment and trained paramedics inside. Ambulances have flashing lights and loud sirens so other vehicles move out of the way.

items.name: Electric Car
items.text: An electric car runs entirely on electricity stored in large batteries. Instead of a petrol engine, it has an electric motor that is silent and smooth. Electric cars produce no exhaust fumes. So they are much better for the environment.

items.name: Hovercraft
items.text: A hovercraft is a machine that moves on a cushion of air. Powerful fans blow air underneath, lifting the craft off the surface. Hovercrafts can travel over water, land, mud, ice, and even swamps!

items.name: Cable Car
items.text: A cable car is a machine that hangs from a steel cable and moves up steep hills or mountains. The cable is powered by a motor at one end that pulls it along. Cable cars are used where roads are too steep for normal vehicles.

items.name: Fire Engine
items.text: A fire engine is a large machine used by firefighters to put out fires. It carries a huge tank of water and a powerful pump. The long ladder on top can extend many floors high to reach trapped people.

items.name: Spacecraft
items.text: A spacecraft is a machine designed to travel in outer space. It can carry humans or fly without a crew. Controlled from Earth. The first spacecraft, Sputnik 1, was launched by the Soviet Union in 1957.

items.name: Cheetah
items.text: The cheetah is the fastest land animal on Earth. It can run at speeds up to 120 km per hour in short bursts. Cheetahs have spotted fur that helps them hide in the grass.

items.name: Elephant
items.text: Elephants are the largest land animals on Earth. They have long trunks used for breathing, smelling, drinking, and picking things up. An elephant's trunk has over 40,000 muscles!

items.name: Dolphin
items.text: Dolphins are highly smart marine mammals. They are not fish. They breathe air through a blowhole on top of their head. Dolphins live in oceans, seas, and some rivers around the world.

items.name: Penguin
items.text: Penguins are birds that cannot fly but are excellent swimmers. People use their flippers to glide gracefully through the water. Most penguins live in the cold Southern Hemisphere, especially Antarctica.

items.name: Tiger
items.text: The tiger is the largest wild cat in the world. Tigers are known for their beautiful orange fur with black stripes. Every tiger has a unique stripe pattern. Just like human fingerprints!

items.name: Eagle
items.text: Eagles are large, powerful birds of prey with incredible eyesight. They can spot a rabbit from over 3 kilometres away! Eagles build huge nests known as eyries. Often on cliff edges or tall trees.

items.name: Octopus
items.text: The octopus is one of the most smart creatures in the ocean. It has eight arms lined with powerful suckers for gripping. Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood!

items.name: Polar Bear
items.text: Polar bears are the world's largest land predators. They live in the Arctic. One of the coldest places on Earth. Polar bears have thick white fur and a layer of fat to keep them warm.

items.name: Whale
items.text: Whales are the largest animals that have ever existed on Earth. The Blue Whale can grow up to 30 metres long and weigh 200 tonnes! Whales are mammals. They breathe air through a blowhole on top of their head.

items.name: Gorilla
items.text: Gorillas are the largest primates on Earth and our close relatives. They live in the forests of central Africa. Gorillas live in family groups led by a dominant male known as a silverback.

items.name: Honey Bee
items.text: Honey bees are small but very important insects. They pollinate flowers. Helping plants reproduce and make fruit. Without bees, most of our fruits, vegetables, and flowers would disappear!

items.name: Crocodile
items.text: Crocodiles are among the most ancient animals on Earth. They outlived the dinosaurs! They have barely changed in 200 million years. Crocodiles have the strongest bite of any animal in the world.

items.name: Butterfly
items.text: Butterflies are beautiful insects known for their colourful wings. They start life as an egg, hatch as a caterpillar, form a chrysalis, then emerge as a butterfly. This amazing transformation is known as metamorphosis.

items.name: Giraffe
items.text: The giraffe is the tallest animal on Earth. Adults can reach 5 to 6 metres tall! Their long necks help them reach leaves at the tops of trees. Despite the long neck, giraffes have exactly the same number of neck bones as humans. Just 7!

items.name: Banyan Tree
items.text: The Banyan tree is one of the most amazing trees in the world. It starts life as a seed that sprouts on another tree. Over time, its roots hang down and reach the ground.

items.name: Lotus
items.text: The lotus is a beautiful flower that grows in muddy water. Even though it grows in mud, it stays perfectly clean and pure. The lotus is the national flower of India and Egypt.

items.name: Neem Tree
items.text: The neem tree is one of the most useful trees in India and the world. Almost every part of it. Leaves, bark, seeds, and oil. Has a use. Neem leaves are used in traditional medicine to treat fever and skin problems.

items.name: Bamboo
items.text: It is one of the fastest-growing plants on Earth. Some species can grow 90 cm in a single day. You can almost watch it grow! It is actually a type of grass, not a tree.

items.name: Mango Tree
items.text: The mango tree is one of the most beloved trees in India. India is the largest producer of mangoes in the entire world. The mango is the national fruit of India.

items.name: Tulsi
items.text: Tulsi, or holy basil, is one of the most sacred plants in Hinduism. Almost every Hindu home has a tulsi plant in the courtyard or on the balcony. It is worshipped daily. Especially by women who water and pray around it.

items.name: Peepal Tree
items.text: The Peepal tree is one of the most sacred trees in Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. Lord Buddha attained enlightenment while meditating under a Peepal tree. This particular tree is known as the Bodhi tree and is revered worldwide.

items.name: Coconut Tree
items.text: The coconut tree is known as the Tree of Life. Every single part of it is useful. The fruit gives coconut water, coconut milk, coconut oil, and coconut flesh. Coconut oil is used for cooking, hair care, and skin care.

items.name: Sunflower
items.text: Sunflowers are tall, bright yellow flowers that love sunlight. They can grow up to 3 metres tall. Taller than most adults! Sunflowers turn their heads to follow the Sun as it moves across the sky.

items.name: Aloe Vera
items.text: It is a succulent plant known for its thick, fleshy leaves. The clear gel inside the leaves is famous for healing skin burns, cuts, and rashes. It gel is one of the most widely used natural remedies in the world.

items.name: Cactus
items.text: A cactus is a plant that lives in hot, dry deserts. Instead of flat leaves, it has sharp spines to keep safe itself from animals. The thick stem stores water inside, like a water tank.

items.name: Rose
items.text: The rose is one of the most beautiful and beloved flowers in the world. Roses come in many colours. Red, pink, white, yellow, and orange. A red rose is a universal symbol of love and romance.

items.name: Jasmine
items.text: It is a beautiful flowering plant known for its very sweet fragrance. In India, jasmine flowers are known as Mogra or Chameli and are deeply loved. Women wear jasmine garlands in their hair on festivals and different occasions.

items.name: Venus Flytrap
items.text: The Venus Flytrap is one of the most fascinating plants in the world. It eats insects! It is a carnivorous plant. Meaning it gets nutrients by trapping and digesting animals. Its leaves form a pair of hinged lobes with tiny trigger hairs inside.

items.name: Rainbow
items.text: A rainbow appears when sunlight passes through water droplets in the air. The droplets act like tiny prisms and split light into its colours. A rainbow always has seven colours: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.

items.name: Volcano
items.text: A volcano is an opening in the Earth's surface through which hot melted rock erupts. The melted rock underground is known as magma. Once it erupts, it is known as lava. Lava can reach temperatures over 1,000 degrees Celsius.

items.name: Tornado
items.text: A tornado is a powerful spinning column of air that reaches from a storm cloud to the ground. It can spin at speeds of over 480 km per hour. Tornadoes are also known as twisters.

items.name: Thunder and Lightning
items.text: It happen during powerful thunderstorms. Lightning is a giant spark of electricity in the sky. It can occur inside a cloud, between clouds, or between a cloud and the ground.

items.name: Glacier
items.text: A glacier is a very big, slow-moving river of ice that forms over thousands of years. Glaciers form when more snow falls than melts, compressing into thick ice. They cover about 10% of Earth's land surface. Mostly in Antarctica and Greenland.

items.name: Monsoon
items.text: The monsoon is a seasonal wind system that brings heavy rainfall to large parts of Asia. In India, the monsoon arrives around June and lasts until September. It brings about 80% of India's annual rainfall. Essential for farming and water supply.

items.name: Earthquake
items.text: An earthquake is a sudden shaking of the ground caused by movement in the Earth's crust. The Earth's outer layer is made of giant pieces known as tectonic plates. When these plates push, pull, or slide against each other. Energy is released as shaking.

items.name: Tsunami
items.text: A tsunami is a series of enormous ocean waves caused by an underwater earthquake or volcano. The name tsunami comes from Japanese and means harbour wave. In the deep ocean, a tsunami wave may only be 30 cm high. Nearly invisible.

items.name: Ocean
items.text: The ocean covers about 71% of Earth's surface. Our planet is mostly water! There are five oceans: the Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, and Arctic. The Pacific Ocean is the largest. It is bigger than all land on Earth combined!

items.name: Desert
items.text: A desert is a landscape that receives very little rainfall. Less than 250 mm per year. Deserts cover about one-third of Earth's land surface. The Sahara in Africa is the world's largest hot desert. As big as the USA!

items.name: Forest
items.text: A forest is a large area of land covered with trees and other plants. Forests cover about 31% of Earth's land surface. The Amazon Rainforest in South America is the world's largest tropical forest.

items.name: Waterfall
items.text: A waterfall is formed when a river flows over a sudden drop in the landscape. The water plunges down, creating a spectacular sight and thundering sound. Angel Falls in Venezuela is the world's highest waterfall. Nearly 1 km tall!

items.name: Cave
items.text: A cave is a natural underground hollow in rock, usually formed by water or lava. Over thousands of years, water dissolves limestone rock, creating tunnels and chambers. The longest cave system in the world is Mammoth Cave in the USA. Over 660 km of passages!

items.name: Aurora
items.text: The Aurora Borealis, or Northern Lights, is one of nature's most breathtaking displays. Colourful ribbons of green, red, blue, and purple dance across the polar night sky. They occur when charged particles from the Sun strike gases in Earth's upper atmosphere.

items.name: Tirupati Balaji
items.text: It is one of the most visited temples in the entire world. It is found on the Tirumala hills in Andhra Pradesh, India. The temple is dedicated to Lord Venkateswara, a form of Lord Vishnu.

items.name: Kashi Vishwanath
items.text: It is one of the holiest temples in Hinduism. It is found in Varanasi (also known as Kashi), Uttar Pradesh, India. The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva. Vishwanath means Lord of the Universe.

items.name: Badrinath Temple
items.text: It is one of the four sacred dhams (holy sites) of Hinduism. It is found high in the Himalayan mountains of Uttarakhand, India. The temple is dedicated to Lord Vishnu in his form as Badrinarayan.

items.name: Meenakshi Amman Temple
items.text: It is a beautiful temple in Madurai, Tamil Nadu. It is dedicated to Goddess Meenakshi (Parvati) and Lord Sundareswara (Shiva). The temple complex has 14 grand towers known as gopurams, covered with colourful sculptures.

items.name: Somnath Temple
items.text: It is one of the twelve Jyotirlingas. The holiest shrines of Lord Shiva. It is found on the western coast of Gujarat, facing the Arabian Sea. Somnath has been attacked and destroyed multiple times throughout history. And rebuilt each time.

items.name: Jagannath Temple Puri
items.text: Jagannath Temple in Puri, Odisha, is one of the four sacred Char Dham pilgrimage sites. It is dedicated to Lord Jagannath. A form of Lord Vishnu. The temple's towering spire rises 65 metres high and can be seen from far away.

items.name: Kedarnath Temple
items.text: It is one of the most sacred and dramatic temples in India. It is found at an altitude of 3,583 metres in the Himalayas of Uttarakhand. The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva and is one of the twelve Jyotirlingas.

items.name: Rameshwaram Temple
items.text: It is one of the four sacred Char Dham pilgrimage sites of Hinduism. It is found on Pamban Island in Tamil Nadu, connected to the mainland by a bridge. The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva. Lord Rama himself is said to have worshipped here.

items.name: Golden Temple Amritsar
items.text: The Golden Temple, or Harmandir Sahib, is the holiest shrine in Sikhism. It is found in Amritsar, Punjab, India. The temple is covered in real gold. Given by Maharaja Ranjit Singh in the 19th century.

items.name: Brihadeeswarar Temple
items.text: It is one of the greatest temples ever built in India. It is found in Thanjavur, Tamil Nadu, and was built by the great Chola king Raja Raja I. It was completed in 1010 CE. Over 1,000 years ago!

items.name: Vaishno Devi Temple
items.text: It is one of the most visited pilgrimage sites in India. It is found in the Trikuta Mountains of Jammu and Kashmir. The temple is dedicated to Goddess Vaishno Devi. A form of the divine mother.

items.name: Lotus Temple Delhi
items.text: The Lotus Temple in New Delhi is one of the most beautiful modern temples in the world. It is a Bahai House of Worship. Open to people of all religions and backgrounds. The building is shaped like a giant white lotus flower with 27 petals made of marble.

items.name: Akshardham Temple
items.text: It in New Delhi is one of the most spectacular temples in the modern world. It was built by the BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha and inaugurated in 2005. The main monument is made of pink sandstone and white marble with no steel or concrete used!

items.name: Dwarka Temple
items.text: Dwarka Temple, or Dwarkadhish Temple, is one of the four sacred Char Dham pilgrimage sites. It is found in Dwarka, Gujarat, on the western coast of India. The temple is dedicated to Lord Krishna. Dwarka was his kingdom in ancient times.

items.name: Lord Ganesha
items.text: It is one of the most beloved gods in Hinduism. He has the head of an elephant and a kind, chubby human body. Ganesha is the son of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati.

items.name: Goddess Lakshmi
items.text: It is the Hindu goddess of wealth, fortune, and beauty. She is the beloved consort of Lord Vishnu. Lakshmi is shown seated on a lotus flower, with gold coins flowing from her hands.

items.name: Lord Krishna
items.text: It is one of the most beloved avatars of Lord Vishnu. He was born in Mathura and grew up in the village of Vrindavan. Krishna is famous for his mischievous childhood. Stealing butter and playing the flute.

items.name: Lord Hanuman
items.text: It is one of the greatest devotees of Lord Rama. He is known for his immense strength, courage, and devotion. Hanuman is the son of Vayu. The god of wind.

items.name: Lord Shiva
items.text: It is one of the most powerful gods in Hinduism. Part of the holy Trinity. He is the destroyer and transformer in the cycle of creation, preservation, and destruction. Shiva lives on Mount Kailash in the Himalayas, deep in meditation.

items.name: Goddess Durga
items.text: It is the fierce and powerful mother goddess of Hinduism. She was created by the combined energy of all the gods to defeat the demon Mahishasura. Durga has eight or ten arms, each holding a divine weapon.

items.name: Lord Brahma
items.text: It is the creator god in Hinduism. Part of the divine Trinity (Trimurti). He created the universe, all living beings, and the four Vedas. Brahma has four heads, each facing a different direction. Symbolising his all-knowing nature.

items.name: Goddess Saraswati
items.text: It is the Hindu goddess of knowledge, learning, arts, and music. She is the consort of Lord Brahma and is associated with pure wisdom. Saraswati is shown wearing pure white. Symbolising the purity of knowledge.

items.name: Lord Vishnu
items.text: It is the preserver in the Hindu Trinity. The one who maintains balance in the universe. He is known as the protector of dharma. Righteous order. Vishnu has four arms holding a conch, discus (Sudarshana Chakra), mace, and lotus.

items.name: Lord Murugan
items.text: It is one of the most beloved gods in South India, especially in Tamil Nadu. He is the son of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati and the younger brother of Lord Ganesha. Murugan is the god of war, victory, wisdom, and beauty.

items.name: Goddess Kali
items.text: It is one of the most powerful and fearsome forms of the divine mother. She represents the destroyer of evil and the liberator of souls. Kali is shown with dark blue skin, a garland of skulls, and a sword in her hand.

items.name: Lord Indra
items.text: It is the king of the gods (Devas) in Hindu mythology. He is the god of thunder, lightning, storms, and rain. Indra wields the Vajra. A divine thunderbolt weapon. As his signature weapon.

items.name: Lord Rama
items.text: It is the seventh avatar of Lord Vishnu and the hero of the Ramayana. He was born as the eldest son of King Dasharatha of Ayodhya. Rama is known as Maryada Purushottam. The ideal man who always follows dharma.

items.name: Goddess Parvati
items.text: It is the divine mother in Hinduism and the consort of Lord Shiva. She is the goddess of love, fertility, devotion, and divine strength. Parvati is the gentle, beautiful form of the supreme mother goddess.

items.name: Diwali
items.text: It is the most popular festival in India and is known as the Festival of Lights. It is celebrated every year in October or November. People light thousands of oil lamps and candles to fill their homes with light.

items.name: Holi
items.text: It is the festival of colours, celebrated in spring across India. People throw bright coloured powders and water at each other. Everyone is covered in pink, red, green, yellow, and blue by the end!

items.name: Navratri
items.text: It means nine nights and is a festival celebrated for nine days. It is dedicated to Goddess Durga and her nine forms. Each day, a different form of Durga is worshipped with different colours and prayers.

items.name: Raksha Bandhan
items.text: It is a beautiful festival celebrating the bond between brothers and sisters. The name means the bond of protection. On this day, a sister ties a colourful thread known as a rakhi on her brother's wrist.

items.name: Ganesh Chaturthi
items.text: It is one of the most joyous festivals in India. It celebrates the birthday of Lord Ganesha. The elephant-headed god of wisdom. Beautifully decorated clay idols of Ganesha are installed in homes and public spaces.

items.name: Pongal
items.text: It is a harvest festival celebrated in Tamil Nadu in January. The name Pongal means to boil or overflow. Referring to the boiling of the harvest rice. The festival lasts for four days. Bhogi, Thai Pongal, Mattu Pongal, and Kaanum Pongal.

items.name: Onam
items.text: It is the harvest festival of Kerala and one of the most celebrated festivals in South India. It falls in the Malayalam month of Chingam (August to September). It celebrates the return of the mythical King Mahabali to visit his beloved people.

items.name: Ugadi
items.text: It is the New Year festival celebrated in Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, and Karnataka. The name Ugadi means the start of a new age in Sanskrit. It falls on the first day of the Chaitra month. Usually in March or April.

items.name: Janmashtami
items.text: It is the joyous celebration of the birth of Lord Krishna. Krishna was born at midnight. So celebrations reach their peak at the stroke of twelve! Temples are beautifully decorated and filled with devotional songs and dances.

items.name: Maha Shivaratri
items.text: It is one of the most important festivals dedicated to Lord Shiva. The name means the Great Night of Shiva. Celebrated on the 14th night of the dark moon in Phalguna. Devotees fast throughout the day and night and stay awake in prayer and devotion.

items.name: Maha Shivaratri
items.text: It is one of the most important festivals dedicated to Lord Shiva. The name means the Great Night of Shiva. Celebrated on the 14th night of the dark moon in Phalguna. Devotees fast throughout the day and night and stay awake in prayer and devotion.

items.name: Christmas
items.text: It is celebrated on 25th December every year to mark the birth of Jesus Christ. Christians believe Jesus was the Son of God, born to bring love and salvation to the world. It is celebrated with joy, lights, decorations, and the exchange of gifts.

items.name: Baisakhi
items.text: It is a harvest festival celebrated mainly in Punjab on April 13 or 14 every year. It marks the beginning of the Sikh New Year and the harvest of the Rabi crop. On this day in 1699, Guru Gobind Singh founded the Khalsa. The community of baptised Sikhs.

items.name: Gudi Padwa
items.text: It is the New Year festival celebrated in Maharashtra, Goa, and parts of Karnataka. It falls on the first day of the Hindu month of Chaitra. Usually March or April. A Gudi is a bright cloth tied to a bamboo pole, topped with a copper pot and neem leaves.

items.name: Matsya Avatar
items.text: Matsya is the first avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a giant fish. The name Matsya means fish in Sanskrit. In this form, Vishnu saved the first man, Manu, from a great flood.

items.name: Kurma Avatar
items.text: Kurma is the second avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a giant tortoise. Kurma means tortoise in Sanskrit. The gods and demons once worked together to churn the cosmic ocean to get Amrita. The nectar of immortality.

items.name: Varaha Avatar
items.text: Varaha is the third avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a mighty boar. The demon Hiranyaksha had stolen the Earth (Bhudevi) and hidden her in the cosmic ocean. Vishnu took the form of a very big boar and dived into the primordial waters.

items.name: Narasimha Avatar
items.text: Narasimha is the fourth avatar of Lord Vishnu. Half man, half lion. Nara means man and Simha means lion in Sanskrit. The demon king Hiranyakashipu had a boon that he could not be killed by man or animal.

items.name: Vamana Avatar
items.text: Vamana is the fifth avatar of Lord Vishnu in the form of a small dwarf Brahmin. The demon king Bali was very powerful and had conquered the three worlds. Bali was generous. He never refused anyone who came to him for a gift.

items.name: Parashurama Avatar
items.text: Parashurama is the sixth avatar of Lord Vishnu. A fierce warrior-sage. The name Parashu means axe. Parashurama means Rama with an axe. He was born as a Brahmin but was a mighty warrior by the will of Vishnu.

items.name: Rama Avatar
items.text: Rama is the seventh and most celebrated avatar of Lord Vishnu. He is the hero of the Ramayana. One of India's greatest epics. Rama was born as the prince of Ayodhya, son of King Dasharatha.

items.name: Krishna Avatar
items.text: Krishna is the eighth and most beloved avatar of Lord Vishnu. He appeared in an age of great war and confusion to restore dharma. Krishna was born in a prison to save the world from the evil king Kansa.

items.name: Buddha Avatar
items.text: The Buddha is considered the ninth avatar of Lord Vishnu in some Hindu traditions. Siddhartha Gautama was born as a prince in Lumbini (modern-day Nepal) around 563 BCE. He left his palace after seeing old age, sickness, and death for the first time.

items.name: Kalki Avatar
items.text: Kalki is the tenth and final avatar of Lord Vishnu. But he has not yet appeared! Hindu scriptures say Kalki will come at the end of the Kali Yuga. The current age of darkness. He will ride a white horse, carry a blazing sword, and destroy all evil from the world.

items.name: The Bhagavad Gita
items.text: It is one of the most important scriptures in Hinduism. It is part of the great epic Mahabharata. The Gita is a conversation between Prince Arjuna and his charioteer Lord Krishna.

items.name: Karma Yoga
items.text: It is one of the paths taught in the Bhagavad Gita. Karma means action or deed. Krishna teaches that every action we do has a consequence. Good actions bring good results.

items.name: Arjuna's Dilemma
items.text: At the start of the Bhagavad Gita, Prince Arjuna faces a terrible problem. He must fight in a war. But the enemies are his own cousins, uncles, and teachers. Arjuna's hands tremble and he puts down his bow.

items.name: Gyaana Yoga
items.text: It is the path of knowledge. One of the four paths in the Bhagavad Gita. It means seeking deep understanding of the self and the universe. Krishna teaches that the highest knowledge is knowing the soul (Atman) is part of God (Brahman).

items.name: Bhakti Yoga
items.text: It is the path of devotion. Loving God with all your heart. In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says Bhakti Yoga is the easiest and most direct path to God. Bhakti means deep, pure love for the divine.

items.name: The Eternal Soul
items.text: One of the most important teachings in the Bhagavad Gita is about the soul. Krishna tells Arjuna: the soul (Atman) can never be killed. Weapons cannot cut it, fire cannot burn it, water cannot wet it, and wind cannot dry it.

items.name: Nishkama Karma
items.text: It is one of the most powerful teachings of the Bhagavad Gita. Nishkama means desireless. Doing something without expecting a reward. Krishna teaches: You have a right to perform your actions, but never to the fruits of those actions.

items.name: Raja Yoga
items.text: It is the path of meditation and mental discipline in the Bhagavad Gita. Raja means king. And Raja Yoga makes you the master (king) of your own mind. Krishna teaches that the mind is our greatest friend. And also our greatest enemy.

items.name: Dharma
items.text: It is one of the central concepts in the Bhagavad Gita and in all of Hindu thought. The name dharma has many meanings. Duty, righteousness, law, and the right way of living. Krishna tells Arjuna: it is better to follow your own dharma imperfectly than another's dharma perfectly.

items.name: The Battlefield Lesson
items.text: The Bhagavad Gita begins on a battlefield. And this setting has a deep meaning. Life itself is a kind of battlefield where we face difficult choices every day. Just as Arjuna faced his own relatives as enemies, we sometimes face conflicts with those we love.

items.name: The Pandavas
items.text: It is the five heroic brothers at the heart of the Mahabharata. Their names are: Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva. They were the sons of King Pandu of Hastinapura.

items.name: The Kauravas
items.text: It is the one hundred sons of King Dhritarashtra of Hastinapura. Duryodhana was the eldest and most powerful of the Kauravas. It were the rivals and cousins of the Pandavas.

items.name: Battle of Kurukshetra
items.text: The Battle of Kurukshetra is the great war at the centre of the Mahabharata. It was fought between the Pandavas and the Kauravas. Two branches of the same family. The battle lasted for 18 days on the field of Kurukshetra in Haryana, India.

items.name: Draupadi
items.text: It is one of the most powerful women in the Mahabharata. She was born from a sacred fire during a yagna performed by her father, King Drupada. It was the wife of all five Pandava brothers.

items.name: Karna
items.text: It is one of the most tragic and admired heroes of the Mahabharata. He was actually the eldest son of Kunti. Making him the Pandavas' own brother! But Kunti gave him up as a baby, and he was raised by a charioteer named Adhiratha.

items.name: Bhishma
items.text: It is one of the greatest and most tragic figures of the Mahabharata. His original name was Devavrata. Son of King Shantanu and the goddess Ganga. He is known as Bhishma because of his terrible vow: he swore never to marry or become king.

items.name: Abhimanyu
items.text: It is one of the most heroic young warriors in the Mahabharata. He was the son of the great archer Arjuna and Subhadra, Krishna's sister. It learned the art of entering the Chakravyuha (a circular military formation) while in the womb!

items.name: Vidura
items.text: It is one of the wisest and most virtuous characters of the Mahabharata. He was the half-brother of Dhritarashtra and Pandu. Born of a servant woman. Despite his lower birth, Vidura became the chief minister of Hastinapura.

items.name: Shakuni
items.text: It was the maternal uncle of the Kauravas and one of the most cunning characters in the Mahabharata. He was the prince of Gandhara (present-day Afghanistan and Pakistan). It carried a deep grudge against the Kuru dynasty for the suffering of his family.

items.name: Drona
items.text: Drona, also known as Dronacharya, was the greatest teacher of warfare in the Mahabharata. He taught both the Pandavas and Kauravas the art of archery and military strategy. Drona's favourite student was Arjuna. He believed Arjuna would become the greatest archer in the world.

items.name: Lord Rama
items.text: It is the hero of the Ramayana and the seventh avatar of Lord Vishnu. He was born as the eldest son of King Dasharatha of Ayodhya. Rama is known as Maryada Purushottam. The ideal man who always followed dharma.

items.name: Sita
items.text: It is the beloved wife of Lord Rama and the heroine of the Ramayana. She was found as a baby in a furrow of the earth by King Janaka. Hence known as Janaki. It is worshipped as an ideal of purity, love, courage, and devotion.

items.name: Ravana
items.text: It was the powerful king of Lanka and the main villain of the Ramayana. He had ten heads and twenty arms, symbolising his vast knowledge and power. It was actually a great scholar. He knew the Vedas and played the veena beautifully.

items.name: Hanuman's Leap to Lanka
items.text: One of the greatest moments in the Ramayana is when Hanuman leaps across the ocean to Lanka. Sita had been kidnapped by Ravana and Rama needed to know where she was. Hanuman volunteered to search for Sita.

items.name: Lakshmana
items.text: It is the devoted younger brother and constant companion of Lord Rama. When Rama was sent into exile, Lakshmana refused to stay behind. He chose to leave the comfort of the palace and follow Rama into the forest out of pure love.

items.name: Vibhishana
items.text: It was the younger brother of Ravana. But unlike his brother, he was righteous and good. He repeatedly advised Ravana to return Sita and avoid war with Rama. But Ravana, blinded by pride, refused to listen.

items.name: Ayodhya
items.text: It is the sacred birthplace of Lord Rama and one of the holiest cities in Hinduism. The name Ayodhya means a city that cannot be conquered. A city of peace. It is found on the banks of the Sarayu river in Uttar Pradesh, India.

items.name: Jatayu
items.text: It is one of the most moving and heroic characters in the Ramayana. He was a mighty eagle. The king of vultures. And an old friend of King Dasharatha. When Ravana was flying back to Lanka with the kidnapped Sita, Jatayu spotted them.

items.name: Sugriva
items.text: It is the monkey king who became one of Rama's most important allies in the Ramayana. He was the younger brother of the mighty Vali, who had unfairly driven him away from his kingdom. It and Rama made a pact. Rama would help him defeat Vali, and Sugriva would help find Sita.

items.name: The Ram Setu
items.text: The Ram Setu, or Adam's Bridge, is a chain of limestone shoals connecting India and Sri Lanka. According to the Ramayana, it was built by Rama's army of monkeys to cross over to Lanka. The bridge is known as Nala Setu after the engineer monkey Nala, son of Vishwakarma, who designed it.

items.name: Valmiki
items.text: It is one of the greatest sages in Hindu tradition. He is known as the Adi Kavi. The first poet in Sanskrit literature. It composed the Ramayana. One of the longest and most beloved epics in the world.

items.name: Adi Shankaracharya
items.text: It was one of the greatest philosophers and saints of India. He was born in Kaladi, Kerala, around 788 CE. By the age of 8, he had mastered all the Vedas and ancient scriptures!

items.name: Mirabai
items.text: It was a great saint-poetess from Rajasthan who lived in the 1500s. From childhood, she was completely devoted to Lord Krishna. She saw him as her true husband. She was a princess who married a Rajput king, but her heart always belonged to Krishna.

items.name: Kabir
items.text: It was a great poet-saint of the 15th century whose teachings touched both Hindus and Muslims. He was born in Varanasi and raised by a Muslim weaver family. It believed that God is one. Whether you call him Rama or Allah.

items.name: Swami Vivekananda
items.text: It was one of the greatest spiritual leaders and thinkers of modern India. He was born Narendranath Datta in Kolkata in 1863. He became the most famous disciple of the great mystic Sri Ramakrishna Paramahamsa.

items.name: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa
items.text: It was one of the greatest mystics and saints of 19th-century India. He was born in 1836 in a village in West Bengal. From childhood he was deeply drawn to the divine. He would go into trances of ecstasy.

items.name: Tukaram
items.text: It was a great Bhakti saint from Maharashtra who lived in the 17th century. He was a devotee of Lord Vitthal (Vishnu) at Pandharpur. It was a simple farmer who experienced great personal tragedy. Losing his first wife and son in a famine.

items.name: Ramanujacharya
items.text: It was a great Vaishnava saint and philosopher of the 11th to 12th century. He was born in Sriperumbudur near Chennai, Tamil Nadu. Ramanuja taught Vishishtadvaita. The philosophy that the soul and God are distinct but always connected.

items.name: Narada Muni
items.text: It is one of the most fascinating and important sages in Hindu mythology. He is the son of Lord Brahma and moves freely between the three worlds. Earth, heaven, and the underworld. Narada is always depicted with a veena (string instrument), chanting the name of Narayana (Vishnu).

items.name: Vyasa
items.text: Vyasa, also known as Veda Vyasa, is one of the most important sages in all of Hindu tradition. He is credited with compiling and organising the four Vedas. The oldest scriptures in Hinduism. It also composed the Mahabharata. The longest epic poem ever written, with over 100,000 verses!

comprehension.subject: Honeybees
comprehension.text: Honeybees are amazing little insects that live together in large groups called colonies. A colony can have up to 60,000 bees! Every bee in the colony has a job to do. The queen bee lays all the eggs. Worker bees collect nectar from flowers and bring it back to the hive. They turn the nectar into honey by fanning it with their wings to dry it out. Bees also help flowers grow by carrying pollen from one flower to another — this is called pollination. Without bees, many of the fruits and vegetables we eat would not exist. So the next time you see a bee buzzing around a flower, remember — it is doing very important work!

questions.question: What do worker bees collect from flowers?
How do bees turn nectar into honey?
options: By fanning it with their wings
questions.correct: By fanning it with their wings

questions.question: What is it called when bees carry pollen between flowers?
What is the queen bee's main job?

comprehension.subject: The Moon
comprehension.text: The Moon is Earth's closest neighbour in space. It is a big ball of rock that travels around the Earth. The Moon does not make its own light — it reflects light from the Sun, which is why it glows at night. The Moon is much smaller than the Earth — you could fit about 50 Moons inside the Earth! As the Moon travels around us, we see different shapes of it — these are called phases. Sometimes we see a full circle called a full moon. Sometimes we see just a thin curved shape called a crescent. There is no air or water on the Moon, so nothing can live there. In 1969, astronaut Neil Armstrong became the first human to walk on the Moon. He said it felt like walking on soft sand!

questions.question: Where does the Moon get its light from?
options: It reflects light from the Sun
questions.correct: It reflects light from the Sun

questions.question: What are the different shapes of the Moon called?
Who was the first human to walk on the Moon?
What is a crescent?
options: A thin curved shape of the Moon
A Moon phase seen only in winter
The dark side of the Moon
questions.correct: A thin curved shape of the Moon

comprehension.subject: Dinosaurs
comprehension.text: Dinosaurs lived on Earth millions of years ago. Some dinosaurs were as small as chickens, while others were taller than buildings! Plant-eating dinosaurs used long necks to reach leaves high in trees. Meat-eating dinosaurs had sharp teeth and strong legs for hunting. Scientists who study dinosaurs are called paleontologists. They learn about dinosaurs by digging up fossils, which are the preserved bones or footprints of ancient animals. No people ever saw real dinosaurs because they disappeared long before humans existed.

questions.question: What do scientists use to learn about dinosaurs?
What is a scientist who studies dinosaurs called?
What did meat-eating dinosaurs use for hunting?
Did humans live with dinosaurs?

comprehension.subject: Frogs
comprehension.text: Frogs are small animals that can live both in water and on land. They begin life as tiny eggs in ponds or lakes. The eggs hatch into tadpoles, which have tails and live underwater. As tadpoles grow, they develop legs and lose their tails until they become frogs. Frogs use their long, sticky tongues to catch insects like flies and mosquitoes. Many frogs can jump very far because they have strong back legs. Some frogs are brightly coloured to warn predators to stay away.

questions.question: What do frog eggs hatch into?
What do frogs use to catch insects?
Why can frogs jump far?
Where can frogs live?
options: Both in water and on land
questions.correct: Both in water and on land

comprehension.subject: Mountains
comprehension.text: Mountains are very tall landforms that rise high above the ground around them. Some mountains are covered with snow all year because the air at the top is very cold. People climb mountains for adventure and to enjoy beautiful views. Many animals, such as goats and eagles, live in mountain areas. Rivers often begin in mountains when snow and ice melt into water. The tallest mountain in the world is Mount Everest, which is located in Asia.

questions.question: Why are some mountain tops covered in snow all year?
What often begins in mountains?
Why do people climb mountains?
What is the tallest mountain in the world?

comprehension.subject: Robots
comprehension.text: Robots are machines that can perform tasks automatically. Some robots work in factories building cars, while others help doctors in hospitals. There are even robots that explore space and the deep ocean where humans cannot easily go. Robots follow instructions from computer programs. Some robots can move, talk, or recognise objects using cameras and sensors. Although robots can do many useful jobs, they still need humans to design, build, and control them.

questions.question: Where do some robots work?
What do robots follow?
Why do robots explore space or the deep ocean?
Who designs and controls robots?

comprehension.subject: Oceans
comprehension.text: Oceans cover most of the Earth's surface and contain salty water. There are five main oceans on Earth. Oceans are home to many kinds of animals, including whales, fish, crabs, and sea turtles. Tiny plants called plankton float in the ocean and help produce much of the oxygen we breathe. Waves are created when wind blows across the water. Oceans are very important because they help control Earth's climate and provide food for millions of people.

questions.question: What kind of water is found in oceans?
What creates waves in the ocean?
What tiny plants help produce oxygen?
Why are oceans important?
options: They help control climate and provide food
questions.correct: They help control climate and provide food

comprehension.subject: The Human Body
comprehension.text: The human body is made up of many different parts that work together. The brain controls thinking, memory, and movement. The heart pumps blood around the body to carry oxygen and nutrients. Lungs help us breathe by taking in oxygen from the air. Bones give the body shape and protect important organs. Muscles help us move, run, jump, and lift things. Eating healthy food, exercising, and sleeping well help keep the body strong and healthy.

questions.question: What does the heart do?
What helps us breathe?
What gives the body shape?
What helps keep the body healthy?

comprehension.subject: Rainforests
comprehension.text: Rainforests are warm, wet forests that receive a lot of rain every year. They are home to many plants and animals, including monkeys, parrots, frogs, and jaguars. Trees in rainforests grow very tall and form a thick roof called a canopy. The canopy blocks much of the sunlight from reaching the ground below. Rainforests help clean the air by absorbing carbon dioxide and producing oxygen. Many medicines used by doctors today come from rainforest plants.

questions.question: What kind of weather do rainforests have?
What is the thick roof of trees called?
Why are rainforests important?
options: They absorb carbon dioxide and produce oxygen
questions.correct: They absorb carbon dioxide and produce oxygen

questions.question: What can come from rainforest plants?

comprehension.subject: Camels
comprehension.text: Camels are animals that are well adapted to living in hot deserts. They can travel long distances without drinking water. A camel stores fat in its hump, which gives it energy when food is hard to find. Camels also have long eyelashes and nostrils that can close to keep out blowing sand. Their wide feet help them walk easily on soft desert sand. People in desert areas often use camels to carry goods and transport people.

questions.question: Where do camels usually live?
What does a camel store in its hump?
Why do camels have long eyelashes?
What helps camels walk on sand?

comprehension.subject: Electricity
comprehension.text: Electricity is a form of energy that powers many things we use every day. Lights, televisions, computers, and refrigerators all need electricity to work. Electricity can come from power stations that use wind, water, sunlight, or fuel to create energy. Wires carry electricity safely into homes and schools. It is important to be careful around electricity because it can be dangerous. People should never touch broken wires or use electrical items with wet hands.

questions.question: What does electricity power?
What carries electricity into homes?
Why should people be careful around electricity?
Which of these can help create electricity?

comprehension.subject: Sharks
comprehension.text: Sharks are fish that live in oceans around the world. They have existed for millions of years, even before dinosaurs disappeared. Sharks have strong bodies and sharp teeth that help them catch food. Some sharks are very large, like the whale shark, while others are quite small. Sharks can smell tiny amounts of blood in the water from far away. Most sharks are not dangerous to humans and usually avoid people. Sharks are important because they help keep ocean ecosystems balanced.

questions.question: Where do sharks live?
What helps sharks catch food?
Are most sharks dangerous to humans?
Why are sharks important?

comprehension.subject: Bullying
comprehension.text: Bullying happens when someone keeps being mean or hurting another person on purpose. It can be through words, actions, or by leaving someone out of games. Bullying can make children feel sad, scared, or lonely. If someone is being bullied, they should tell a trusted adult like a parent or teacher. Good friends treat each other with respect and kindness. Standing up for others and including everyone helps stop bullying.

questions.question: What is bullying?
How can bullying make someone feel?
Who should you tell if someone is being bullied?
What do good friends do?

comprehension.subject: Stranger Safety
comprehension.text: Stranger safety means knowing how to stay safe around people you do not know. Children should never go anywhere with a stranger or accept gifts, food, or rides from them without a trusted adult. If a stranger tries to talk to a child in a way that feels uncomfortable, the child should walk away and tell a parent or teacher. Staying close to trusted adults in public places helps keep children safe.

questions.question: What should you do if a stranger offers you a ride?
options: Say no and tell a trusted adult
questions.correct: Say no and tell a trusted adult

questions.question: What should children accept from strangers?
What should you do if you feel uncomfortable?
options: Walk away and tell an adult
questions.correct: Walk away and tell an adult

questions.question: Who helps keep children safe?

comprehension.subject: Online Safety
comprehension.text: Online safety means using the internet in a careful and smart way. Children should never share personal information like their full name, address, school, or phone number online. They should only talk to people they know in real life and avoid strange messages. If something online feels wrong or scary, they should tell a trusted adult immediately. It is also important to balance screen time with outdoor play and rest.

questions.question: What should children NOT share online?
Who should children talk to online?
options: People they know in real life
questions.correct: People they know in real life

questions.question: What should you do if something online feels wrong?
Why is outdoor play important?

comprehension.subject: Lying and Honesty
comprehension.text: Honesty means telling the truth even when it is difficult. Lying is when someone says something that is not true on purpose. Lies can cause problems and make people lose trust in each other. When someone tells the truth, even after making a mistake, it helps fix problems and build trust again. Being honest is an important value that helps friendships and families stay strong.

questions.question: What is honesty?
What is lying?
options: Saying something not true on purpose
questions.correct: Saying something not true on purpose

questions.question: What can lies do?
What helps fix mistakes?

comprehension.subject: Stealing and Respect
comprehension.text: Stealing means taking something that belongs to someone else without permission. It is unfair and can hurt other people’s feelings. Respect means caring about other people’s things and asking before using them. If someone wants to borrow something, they should always ask first and return it safely. Being respectful helps build trust and good friendships.

questions.question: What is stealing?
What should you do before borrowing something?
What does respect mean?
What does stealing do to others?

comprehension.subject: Peer Pressure
comprehension.text: Peer pressure happens when friends or other children try to make someone do something. Sometimes it can be good, like encouraging healthy habits. But sometimes it can be bad, like asking someone to break rules. It is important to make the right choices even if others are doing something wrong. Saying 'no' politely and walking away is a strong and brave choice.

questions.question: What is peer pressure?
Is peer pressure always bad?
What should you do if friends do something wrong?
What is a brave choice?

comprehension.subject: Littering
comprehension.text: Littering means throwing trash on the ground instead of putting it in a bin. It makes places dirty and can harm animals and nature. Clean parks and streets are important for everyone to enjoy. People should always throw rubbish in bins and recycle when possible. Keeping the environment clean shows respect for nature and other people.

questions.question: What is littering?
What can littering harm?
Where should rubbish go?
What does a clean environment show?

comprehension.subject: Cheating
comprehension.text: Cheating means breaking the rules to get an unfair advantage. It can happen in tests, games, or competitions. Cheating is not honest and can hurt others who are trying their best. It is better to try hard and do your own work, even if it is difficult. Learning from mistakes helps children grow stronger and smarter.

questions.question: What is cheating?
Where can cheating happen?
What does cheating do to others?
What is better than cheating?

comprehension.subject: Kindness and Sharing
comprehension.text: Kindness means being helpful, caring, and thoughtful toward others. Sharing means giving part of what you have to someone else. Kind people make others feel happy and included. Sharing toys, food, or time can make friendships stronger. Even small kind actions, like saying nice words or helping someone, can make a big difference.

questions.question: What is kindness?
What does sharing mean?
options: Giving part of what you have
questions.correct: Giving part of what you have

questions.question: How do kind actions affect others?
What can strengthen friendships?
